name: Viking 1
description: The first spacecraft to successfully land on Mars and perform its mission, touching down in Chryse Planitia on July 20, 1976. It operated for over six years, returning thousands of images and atmospheric data.

name: Viking 2
description: Landed in Utopia Planitia on September 3, 1976, at a higher latitude than Viking 1 to investigate northern plains geology and search for seasonal frost. Operated for nearly four years before its batteries failed.

name: Mars Pathfinder
description: Landed in Ares Vallis on July 4, 1997, deploying the Sojourner rover — the first wheeled vehicle to traverse another planet. Demonstrated airbag landing technology and returned over 16,500 images.

name: Spirit (MER-A)
description: Landed in Gusev Crater on January 4, 2004, a site chosen for its potential as a former lakebed. Operated for over six years, discovering silica-rich soil indicative of past hydrothermal activity before becoming stuck in soft soil.

name: Opportunity (MER-B)
description: Landed in Meridiani Planum on January 25, 2004, and drove over 45 km across the Martian surface — the longest extraterrestrial overland journey. Found hematite concretions ('blueberries') confirming ancient liquid water before losing contact in a global dust storm in 2018.

name: Phoenix
description: Landed in the northern polar plains (Vastitas Borealis) on May 25, 2008, directly confirming subsurface water ice by excavating and analyzing soil samples. Observed snowfall from Martian clouds during its 5-month mission.

name: Curiosity
description: Landed in Gale Crater on August 6, 2012, using a novel sky crane system. Has confirmed that Mars once had habitable conditions including liquid water, complex organics, and energy sources for microbial life. Still operational on the slopes of Aeolis Mons.

name: InSight
description: Landed in Elysium Planitia on November 26, 2018, to study Mars's deep interior structure. Detected over 1,300 marsquakes using its seismometer, revealing a layered crust, mantle, and liquid iron core. Mission ended December 2022 due to dust accumulation on solar panels.

name: Perseverance
description: Landed in Jezero Crater on February 18, 2021, a site interpreted as an ancient river delta and lakebed. Deployed the Ingenuity helicopter, collected the first Mars samples for potential future return, and detected oxygen production via MOXIE. Currently operational.

name: Mars 2 Lander
description: The Soviet Mars 2 lander became the first human-made object to reach the Martian surface on November 27, 1971, though it crashed after its descent system failed during a dust storm. The parachute and retrorocket sequence malfunctioned, resulting in impact at high velocity.

name: Mars 3 Lander
description: Achieved the first successful soft landing on Mars on December 2, 1971, transmitting a partial image before going silent 110 seconds after touchdown — likely due to interference from a severe global dust storm. Its descent capsule has been tentatively identified in MRO imagery.

name: Mars 6 Lander
description: The Soviet Mars 6 lander entered the Martian atmosphere on March 12, 1974, transmitting 224 seconds of atmospheric data during descent — the first in-situ measurements of the Martian atmosphere. Contact was lost just before touchdown in the Samara Valles region.

name: Beagle 2
description: The British-built Beagle 2 lander, part of ESA's Mars Express mission, successfully reached the Martian surface in Isidis Planitia on December 25, 2003, but failed to fully deploy its solar panels, preventing communication. Located by MRO imagery in 2015.

name: Mars Polar Lander
description: NASA's Mars Polar Lander was intended to land near the south polar layered terrain on December 3, 1999, to study volatile deposits and climate history. Contact was never established after entry; the most probable cause was premature engine shutdown triggered by spurious landing-leg deployment signals.

name: Zhurong
description: China's Zhurong rover, part of the Tianwen-1 mission, successfully landed in southern Utopia Planitia on May 15, 2021, making China only the second nation to operate a rover on Mars. It surveyed subsurface ice deposits and ancient shoreline features before entering hibernation.

name: Olympus Mons
description: The largest volcano in the solar system, rising 21.9 km above the Martian datum and spanning 610 km at its base — roughly the size of France. Its enormous size results from stationary plate tectonics allowing repeated eruptions over billions of years atop a single hotspot.

name: Valles Marineris
description: A system of canyons stretching 4,000 km along the Martian equator — nearly the width of North America — reaching depths of 7 km and widths of 200 km. It formed primarily through tectonic rifting associated with the Tharsis volcanic bulge, augmented by erosion and collapse.

name: Hellas Planitia
description: The largest confirmed impact crater in the solar system by depth, measuring approximately 2,300 km in diameter and 7.2 km deep below the surrounding terrain. The floor hosts the highest atmospheric pressure on Mars, and shows evidence of ancient glacial, fluvial, and aeolian activity.

name: Ascraeus Mons
description: The northernmost and tallest of the three Tharsis Montes shield volcanoes, rising approximately 18 km above the datum with a summit caldera complex at its peak. Its flank eruptions and radial fracture systems suggest a long and complex magmatic history.

name: Pavonis Mons
description: The middle of the three Tharsis Montes, sitting almost precisely on the Martian equator at an elevation of about 14 km. Its symmetrical profile and deep summit caldera are characteristic of Martian shield volcanism driven by stationary mantle plumes.

name: Arsia Mons
description: The southernmost and largest-volume Tharsis Montes shield volcano, featuring the largest caldera of the three at 110 km in diameter. Evidence suggests it last erupted approximately 150 million years ago and may have hosted a subglacial lake beneath a glacial ice sheet on its northwest flank.

name: Elysium Mons
description: The largest volcano in the Elysium volcanic province, rising about 12.6 km above the datum and situated in the Elysium Planitia region. Unlike the Tharsis volcanoes, Elysium Mons is thought to have formed over a separate mantle upwelling on the eastern hemisphere.

name: Syrtis Major Planum
description: A dark, low-relief shield volcano and the most prominent dark region visible from Earth through a telescope. Its dark appearance results from basaltic rock exposed by wind erosion of reddish dust. Syrtis Major was the first Martian surface feature observed and mapped, by Christiaan Huygens in 1659.

name: Utopia Planitia
description: The largest recognized impact basin on Mars, approximately 3,300 km in diameter, now largely buried and resurfaced. It hosts significant subsurface water-ice deposits detected by SHARAD radar, and is the landing site of both Viking 2 and China's Zhurong rover.

name: Acidalia Planitia
description: A broad northern plains region, one of the darkest areas on Mars, situated north of Chryse Planitia and adjacent to the dichotomy boundary. It shows polygonal surface patterns consistent with subsurface ice and is geologically connected to the outflow channel systems of the Chryse basin.

name: Argyre Planitia
description: A large impact basin approximately 1,800 km in diameter and among the most ancient features on Mars, dating to the Late Heavy Bombardment ~3.9 billion years ago. Argyre shows evidence of glacial erosion and may have hosted a transient liquid water lake fed by meltwater from surrounding highlands.

name: North Polar Cap
description: A permanent polar ice cap approximately 1,000 km across composed of water ice overlaid by a seasonal carbon dioxide frost layer. The surrounding layered terrain (Planum Boreum) preserves a detailed stratigraphic record of Martian climate cycles over millions of years.

name: South Polar Cap
description: A permanent cap of water ice and CO₂ ice (dry ice) approximately 350 km across, smaller than its northern counterpart due to Mars's elliptical orbit placing southern summers near perihelion. MARSIS radar data suggest a possible subglacial liquid water reservoir beneath the layered deposits.
